title: Hardware Realization of a Hamiltonian Simulation Algorithm for Time-Domain Maxwells Equations
authors: Gautam Sharma, Apurva Tiwari, Niladri Gomes, Jezer Jojo, J. Eric Bracken, Jay Pathak
abstract: We present the first quantum-hardware implementation of a Hamiltonian simulation algorithm that produces signed vector-field solutions to the time-domain Maxwells equations using a Schrodingerisation-based approach.The electromagnetic fields are discretized using finite-difference operators, and the resulting non-unitary matrices are mapped to Bell-basis Trotter blocks, enabling efficient circuit construction. We introduce a measurement procedure that retrieves not only field amplitudes, but also physical directions of the electric and magnetic field values at select spatial points. Implementing this logic on quantum hardware relies on relative-phase-based sign reconstruction. Numerical results obtained using IonQ QPU, show good agreement with analytical solutions of benchmark problems in two dimensions and on simulators; in three dimensions. We further extend our approach to compute fields scattered from simple bodies, by enforcing appropriate boundary conditions. Our work lays the foundational steps towards realizing quantum-hardware solutions for computational electromagnetics.
summary: This paper presents the first experimental demonstration of a quantum algorithm capable of computing and measuring time-evolving electromagnetic vector fields on real quantum hardware. By using Schrödingerisation and Bell-basis decomposition, the authors map the non-unitary Maxwell equations onto a unitary quantum circuit. They successfully implemented this on an IonQ trapped-ion processor, using circuit compression techniques to make the depth manageable for NISQ-era devices. The results show that the hardware can accurately capture the temporal evolution and reflections of electromagnetic waves.
main_problem: Developing a practical quantum-hardware implementation for simulating the time-domain Maxwell equations, specifically overcoming the challenges of non-unitary dynamics and the extraction of signed vector-field values.
main_result: The authors demonstrate the first quantum-hardware implementation of a Hamiltonian simulation algorithm for signed vector-field solutions, showing that the IonQ QPU can qualitatively reproduce electromagnetic field evolution.
method: The approach uses Schrödingerisation to transform non-unitary dynamics into a unitary form, Bell-basis decomposition for efficient circuit construction, and ADAPT-AQC for circuit compression, alongside a relative-phase-based sign reconstruction technique.
model_or_system: The system involves electromagnetic field propagation in a homogeneous, lossless, and isotropic medium, discretized on a 3D Yee finite-difference time-domain grid and implemented on an IonQ trapped-ion quantum processor.
key_observables: Magnitudes and physical directions (signs) of the electric (E) and magnetic (H) field components.
important_parameters: Grid dimensions (up to 16x16x16), time steps (dt = 0.1, 0.01), and qubit requirements (n = log2 N + 3).
main_assumptions: The medium is assumed to be homogeneous, lossless, and isotropic; the simulation is source-free; and the boundary conditions are limited to PEC and PMC types.
figures_summary: Figures show snapshots of E and H field evolution in 2D and 3D grids, including reflections from PEC scatterers, and a comparison between classical, noise-free, and hardware-based field values.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from the continuous Maxwell equations to a discrete matrix-based ODE system, introduces the Schrödingerisation and Bell-basis framework, details the hardware implementation and circuit compression, and concludes with numerical validation on IonQ hardware.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and many-body dynamics as it provides a foundational framework for using quantum hardware to solve complex classical electromagnetic problems, potentially bridging the gap between quantum computing and computational electromagnetics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Simulation results for the 2D Maxwell’s equations on an empty 32×32 grid with PMC boundary conditions on all edges and PEC boundaries on top and bottom surfaces. From top to bottom, the rows show the evolution of the field components Ez, Hx, and Hy, respectively. Columns correspond to snapshots at times T = 0, 8, 16, and 24.

Fig. 2: Simulation results for the 2D Maxwell’s equations on a 16 × 16 grid with an internal scatterer and PMC boundary conditions on all outer edges. From top to bottom, the rows show the evolution of the field components Ez, Hx, and Hy, respectively. Columns correspond to snapshots at times T = 0, 5, 10, and 15.

Fig. 3: Three-dimensional evolution of the magnetic-field component Hx shown as an isosurface on a 16 × 16 × 16 grid with PMC boundary conditions. The fields are excited by an initially localized point source in the Ez component at the center of the domain, (x0, y0, z0) = (8, 8, 8).
Fig. 4: Orthogonal two-dimensional cross sections of the Hx field taken through the center of the domain in the xy, xz, and yz planes at times T = 5, 10.

Fig. 5: Comparison of field values measured at the center of the 16×16 computational grid, (8, 8), where the initial electric-field excitation is applied. Time evolution of the field components Ez, Hx, and Hy is shown for classical simulation, noise-free quantum simulation, and quantum hardware execution. The noise-free quantum results closely follow the analytical solution, while the hardware results qualitatively reproduce the correct temporal behavior despite the presence of device noise.
